text: The surgeon uses a Tuo silk suture to create a purse string on the dilated colon at the splenic flexure. Upon insertion, the colon visibly deflates as air is released, with no stool present.

The surgeon traces the bowel to identify the twist, observing the transverse colon leading to the splenic flexure with visible scarring in the mesentery from chronic twisting. The bowel is detorsed, revealing a white attenuated line without retroperitoneal attachments. The surgeon plans a generous splenic flexure resection and decompresses the bowel further for easier manipulation. The camera is positioned in the midline, providing a view of the unsplayed mesentery.

The surgeon examines the mesentery with visible scarring from previous twisting. They mark the planned distal margin on the distal descending colon and begin dissection from a medial position, hugging the bowel wall due to the absence of lateral attachments. The camera is positioned in the patient's right lower quadrant for better visualization. The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect the mesentery and parietal peritoneum, ensuring the resection includes both colotomies. The spleen is visible in the distance, and the splenic flexure is seen curving below its usual position.

The surgeon navigates around the corner of the distal transverse colon, observing the omentum attached to it. The surgeon attempts to enter the lesser sac, focusing on identifying the margin on the transverse colon. The gastrocolic omentum is visible anteriorly, and the surgeon continues to take full thickness with the mesentery while approaching the lesser sac.

The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon, progressing towards the falciform ligament. The transverse colon is unfolded to ensure it lays without tension, and the surgeon marks the transverse colon margin for orientation. The mesentery is dissected at the marked location, completing the separation.

The surgeon examines the resected bowel specimen, pulling it over an instrument to assess its length. The proximal and distal ends of the bowel are divided using a stapler, achieving the division with a single staple load. The transverse colon and distal descending colon, possibly extending to the proximal sigmoid, are aligned for an end-to-end anastomosis. The surgeon uses a stay suture to maintain alignment and begins sewing the staple lines together, ensuring there is no tension in the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a distal colotomy followed by a proximal colotomy, checking the perfusion of the inner lip to ensure it enhances properly. The anastomosis is held stable by the robotic system while the surgeon begins sewing with full thickness bites.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis by sewing towards themselves for the inner layer, ensuring both sutures meet in the middle for secure tying. The posterior suture on the staple line is brought anteriorly and tied to a silk suture. The small bowel is moved aside to inspect the thick mesentery for any bleeding, with cauterization applied as needed. The descending colon is exteriorized through a fan and steel incision, with the surgical field showing no active bleeding.
